chapters.title: The body shapes how we experience being human
chapters.text:
People live inside physical forms, yet we are far more than flesh and bone. Even so, the search for beauty remains a constant pull. Wyatt Maines began thinking about his connection to his own body very early in life. To follow his story, we have to start with his parents and where they came from.

Anyone who looks different from the norm often feels cut off from the world around them.

His mother, Kelly, came from a household where she never had to act proper or refined, and she carried no shame about being born out of wedlock. Her aunt took her in at age two and raised her until she was seventeen. After finishing school and landing a steady job, she met Wayne at a professional seminar. They dated long distance for a year before finally moving in together.

Wayne grew up in a small community and worked a long list of jobs while helping raise his younger siblings. He did not want to spend his whole life doing manual labor, so he pursued an education. He earned a doctorate from West Virginia University, where he crossed paths with Kelly, and they married three years later.

Together they built a life that was more stable than what either of them had growing up, and they decided to start a family. Kelly's cousin became pregnant with identical twins, and the Mainses chose private adoption, welcoming two boys, Wyatt and Jonas, since the cousin was too young to care for them.

From the start, the twins showed different sides of themselves. Jonas loved Star Wars, while Wyatt gravitated toward the mermaid Ariel from The Little Mermaid and other characters that other boys his age usually ignored. One day he told his father that he hated his body and refused to wear the same plaid shirts his brother wore. What Wyatt was feeling had a name, gender dysphoria.

This summary follows Wyatt from toddlerhood through his coming out and transition. The story has plenty of rough patches, but it matters, because it covers picking out clothes, navigating friendships at school, and pushing back against bullying.

chapters.title: Being transgender means a gap between body and identity
chapters.text:
Every morning Kelly wrestled with dressing Wyatt, so she started buying more pink, more clearly feminine pieces. Wayne had doubts, yet he did not stand in her way. Kelly sensed she needed to understand what was going on with her son, so she began reading about homosexuality and transsexualism. The second term seemed to describe adults who changed their bodies through surgery. That description did not quite fit her child.

Eventually, the word transgender kept showing up in her reading. Most people never stop to think about gender, because it feels natural, so it never needs a justification. Kelly had never once had to think about her own.

Gender is about how a person feels on the inside, not what their body looks like on the outside.

Transgender people often feel gender dysphoria, which shows up in a few common ways. They may want to be seen as a gender different from the one they were assigned at birth. They may prefer clothes that match that other gender. They may choose characters and activities tied to that other gender. And they may feel deep discomfort with their sexual anatomy.

The gap between inner gender and outer body causes real distress. Kelly hated seeing Wyatt struggle. He kept asking her when he would finally become a girl. Wayne had trouble understanding what his son was going through. He wanted to help, but he had no idea how.

A useful way to frame it came from researcher Amy Ellis Nutt, who told a doctor that sexual orientation describes who you go to bed with, while gender identity describes who you go to bed as.

Kelly worried about how the world would treat Wyatt. She hoped the college town of Orono, Maine, where they moved for Wayne's job when the boys were four, would be more open. The twins competed constantly, and at one point Wyatt hit Jonas because his brother got to be whoever he wanted. In a journal, Wyatt also wrote a reminder to himself to keep away from kids who picked on others.

After school, Wyatt would swap his shirt and pants for a dress. His parents met somewhere in the middle on clothing. He did not wear skirts to school, but his shoes, lunchbox, and backpack were all pink.

After years of clear signs, Kelly could no longer deny that Wyatt was transgender. Finding a specialist who worked with gender-questioning kids was not easy, but Wyatt stayed hopeful. They connected with Dr. Virginia Holmes, who suggested they take Wyatt's gender expression slowly. She wanted to make sure he was not simply gay. Wyatt had no such doubt. He was a girl.

chapters.title: Transition is how a transgender person steps into who they really are
chapters.text:
When Wyatt asked people to use she and her, the laws around LGBT rights were still harsh, most states did not allow same-sex marriage, and trans issues barely registered in public life.

Before fifth grade, Wyatt had to fill out a form about his goals and how to reach them. The most important one was simply being himself. In 2007, he went through a psychological evaluation. The biggest fear he shared was that people would keep seeing him as a boy. His masculine features filled him with disgust.

Hormones guide the body's sexual development from the womb onward, but the differences between people are not always clean. The straight gyrus, a brain region tied to social awareness, tends to be larger in females, but its size shapes how well we read other people regardless of sex.

The body we are born with and the gender we feel can be two very different things.

Studying gender is complicated because there is no animal model to test on. You cannot simply ask an animal what its gender is. Mutations can also produce chromosome patterns that do not match a person's visible anatomy.

The pressure to label a newborn as one gender has hurt many intersex people, those whose internal and external sex traits do not line up. Doctors often push for surgery right away, while intersex advocates argue for waiting and letting the child show what feels right.

In the last decade, more people have started treating gender as a range rather than a binary. These days, kids can delay puberty with hormone blockers. That option matters a lot, because reshaping a body that has already gone through puberty is much harder and the results are often less satisfying.

Knowing who we are matters deeply. We want to understand ourselves, and at the same time society wants to slot us into a category. Transgender people want the same chance to be themselves, and they need the right support to get there. When Wyatt began his transition, there was only one endocrinologist in the country who specialized in treating transgender children.

A note worth adding. Research has found that a year of female hormones removes the edge that trans athletes who went through a male puberty had over cis women. The hormones often drive testosterone lower than in many cis athletes, since the treatment includes suppressants.

chapters.title: Anyone outside the mainstream has to keep fighting for basic respect
chapters.text:
Norman Spack, Wyatt's endocrinologist, laid out a plan. Start puberty blockers around age twelve, begin hormone replacement around sixteen, and consider surgery after eighteen.

Everyone Wyatt knew treated her as a girl, but as long as she carried a boy's name, something felt unfinished. Eventually, Wyatt picked the name Nicole, but changing a name legally was complicated. Maine law required the new name to be printed in a newspaper. Kelly and Wayne went to court to ask the judge to skip that step. Wayne still struggled to use the new name at home, but he stood up in court and told the judge the family was worried about their child's safety. The judge agreed to their request.

Wayne also worried about his own relatives. They needed time to adjust, but Nicole appreciated that they were trying. In the end, they just wanted her to be happy.

School, though, was not always welcoming. Some parents objected to Nicole using the girls' bathroom, claiming they were worried about their own daughters. One boy threatened Nicole, and other kids called her a slur she had never heard before, though she could tell it was meant to hurt. The school told her to use a gender-neutral bathroom instead.

Kelly and Wayne had to defend Nicole's rights when the Christian Civic League started pushing back against what it called the gay agenda. The bathroom fight ended up on the local news. The press did not name the Mainses, but the entire state was talking about the story. A man named Paul Melanson argued that his son should also have the right to use the girls' bathroom and that Nicole should not get special treatment.

After all the public attention, a school staff member had to walk with Nicole for her own safety. Nicole felt singled out and lonely, sinking into depression and thinking of herself as a freak. The harassment at school made her painfully self-aware.

Transgender kids often become depressed because of the bullying and hatred they take from classmates.

Nicole had to find ways to deal with rejection and isolation, so she started seeing a therapist. The therapist suggested holding eye contact with the kids who bullied her rather than lashing out.

A small cultural side note. Some societies, including communities in the Eastern Highlands of Papua New Guinea and in the Dominican Republic, have long recognized more than two genders. People in India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Nepal had a word for trans people four thousand years ago. The word was hijra.

chapters.title: The road through transition is never smooth
chapters.text:
Plenty of children face discrimination over what they wear or the accessories they carry. Some of those stories end up in court, while others fade out of view. Protecting trans rights under the law is hard, because much of the opposition comes from stereotypes and old-fashioned attitudes.

The Mainses sued for discrimination, claiming Nicole had the right to use the bathroom that matched her gender identity. Lawsuits can stretch on for years, though, and Kelly did not want Nicole to keep dealing with stalking and harassment. Kelly and the kids moved to Portland, and Wayne stayed behind in Orono for work. The whole mess forced the family to scale down, leave their house and friends behind, and search for a little peace.

A thought worth holding. Every choice you make and every thought that crosses your mind is yours. Each moment of your life is shaped by you. That idea comes from writer Amy Ellis Nutt.

Around 2009, while Nicole's female friends were starting puberty, she had to keep hers quiet. She began getting injections to hold off male development. A few years later, at thirteen, she started hormone therapy.

In the meantime, GLAD, the GLBTQ Legal Advocates and Defenders group, put together a legal team for the Mainses. Things were looking up, but the family was on edge, waiting for the next blow.

At the time, Maine was weighing a bill that would let business owners bar anyone from using their restrooms. The issue the family had run from had followed them. Wayne spoke out against the bill in public, with tears in his eyes. He brought Nicole along to talk to lawmakers and try to win their support against the bill.

Hiding who you really are wears you down. We all want to live as our true selves.

The kids were starting at a new school, and the family hoped for a clean slate. Nicole met a girl she liked, and she worried about telling her the truth. Spending another day hiding felt unbearable, so she told her new friend she was transgender. The friend's response was simple. Cool. A wave of relief hit Nicole. She could finally be open about who she was, and she was happy to tell people she was transgender. Her only fear now was that they would stop loving her.

chapters.title: Nicole's fight for rights rippled across the whole country
chapters.text:
Wayne and Nicole's advocacy paid off. The bill did not pass, a win for the Mainses and for the broader transgender community. Later, a reporter from a major newspaper reached out, wanting to write about what it was like to raise a transgender child. After the Boston Globe ran the article, many more media requests followed.

In 2014, a GLAD attorney called Wayne to share the news. The court had ruled in the family's favor. Kelly texted her kids at school, and the room broke into applause when Nicole told her friends.

That ruling meant more than one family's victory. It sent a message that trans kids have rights and that their lives matter. They deserve to be treated fairly.

A year later, a school board in another Maine town introduced a transgender student policy to protect kids from discrimination. Under the new rules, transgender students could use the bathrooms and locker rooms that matched their gender.

Boston University School of Medicine concluded that the most effective way to treat transgender patients was to provide hormone replacement therapy and surgery when needed.

Talk therapy alone is not enough to treat gender dysphoria. Physical changes are often necessary for transgender people to feel at home in their bodies.

Even with those wins, the fight was not over. Insurers still treated sex reassignment surgery as cosmetic. Wayne worked with GLAD to appeal the university's decision, and they won.

All of that fighting was draining. Wayne sometimes felt the family was living on a battlefield.

When the kids got into college, Nicole was asked to play a transgender teenager in an episode of the show Royal Pains. The driver who took Nicole and Wayne to the set said he had turned conservative because the Democrats had pushed for a transgender mascot in college sports. The driver looked embarrassed when he realized Nicole was transgender. Wayne told him that her being a girl had nothing to do with politics. It was about being herself.

Graduation eventually arrived. Kelly and Wayne could hardly believe everything the family had been through. Jonas won an acting scholarship, and Nicole was fully herself, sassy and joyful. Most importantly, they had cleared every hurdle as a family.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Our stories are part of what makes us who we are. We are tied to our bodies, but our stories do not end there. Our bodies carry our identities and help us connect with other people and the world around us.

When it comes to transgender people, those who do not know much about the topic tend to fixate on surgery. Surgery is not the only goal of a transition, but for many people it is a necessary step. Nicole wanted to feel at peace in her own body.

Surgery is not a magic fix, either. Nicole will always know she is different, and she will not be able to carry children. She dreamed of getting married and adopting kids. We are reaching the end of this summary, but the Maines family's story is still unfolding. There are more challenges ahead and more feelings to work through.

Society has moved forward on transgender rights, but the fight is not finished. Many people still face discrimination every day. We still have a long way to go. The most effective way to teach people is by sharing the stories of real people and their varied experiences. Human beings are drawn to narratives. They help us feel empathy and learn about the struggles of others. Share your own story and listen to the stories of others.

A small thing to try.

Do not be afraid to share what you have been through. When you meet resistance, hold on to your courage. Show care and support to your friends and family. You can lift them up and help them find the confidence to tell their own stories. People have far more in common than they have differences, so lead with compassion and connect more deeply with the people you love.
